# Research: subdomain-vs-path-based-club-routing

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** What are the infrastructure and implementation tradeoffs between subdomain-based club URLs (club.app.com) vs path-based (/clubs/:slug) for a self-hosted Node.js API? Which is simpler to ship first given no existing DNS/wildcard cert infrastructure?

---

# Subdomain vs Path-Based Club Routing: Infrastructure & Implementation Tradeoffs

## TL;DR

**Ship path-based routing first.** It requires zero DNS changes, zero certificate work, and zero proxy reconfiguration. Subdomain routing is the better long-term UX, but it adds 3–5 infrastructure layers that have nothing to do with your product logic. Migrate later once clubs are actually using the platform.

---

## 1. The Two Approaches Defined

### Path-Based
```
https://app.com/clubs/the-pines-gc
https://app.com/clubs/riverside-cc/dashboard
https://app.com/clubs/riverside-cc/api/leaderboard
```

### Subdomain-Based
```
https://the-pines-gc.app.com
https://riverside-cc.app.com/dashboard
https://riverside-cc.app.com/api/leaderboard
```

---

## 2. Infrastructure Requirements Side-by-Side

| Layer | Path-Based | Subdomain-Based |
|---|---|---|
| DNS | Single A/CNAME record | Wildcard `*.app.com` A record |
| TLS Certificate | Single cert for `app.com` | Wildcard cert (`*.app.com`) or per-club cert via ACME |
| Reverse Proxy Config | Static, set once | Must route by `Host` header; dynamic if auto-provisioning |
| CORS | One origin | Per-subdomain origin or `*.app.com` wildcard |
| Cookies | `app.com` domain, simple | Must set `Domain=.app.com` or per-subdomain |
| Session/Auth | Trivial | Cross-subdomain sharing requires `Domain=.app.com` cookie scope |
| Local Dev | `localhost:3000/clubs/slug` works immediately | Requires `/etc/hosts` entries or a tool like `dnsmasq` |
| CI/Preview Envs | Works on any preview URL | Preview URLs (e.g., Vercel/Railway) don't support wildcard subdomains |

---

## 3. Path-Based: Deep Dive

### 3.1 Express Routing

```js
// Flat club namespace — all club routes under /clubs/:slug
router.use('/clubs/:slug', async (req, res, next) => {
  const club = await Club.findBySlug(req.params.slug);
  if (!club) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Club not found' });
  req.club = club;
  next();
});

router.get('/clubs/:slug/dashboard', requireClubMember, dashboardHandler);
router.get('/clubs/:slug/leaderboard', leaderboardHandler);
```

### 3.2 Slug Constraints

Slugs should be:
- Lowercase, kebab-case: `the-pines-gc`
- URL-safe: `[a-z0-9-]`, max 63 chars
- Unique at creation time
- Immutable after creation (or handled via redirect chain)

```js
function toSlug(name) {
  return name
    .toLowerCase()
    .trim()
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9\s-]/g, '')
    .replace(/\s+/g, '-')
    .slice(0, 63);
}
```

### 3.3 Reserved Slug Namespace

Block slugs that conflict with top-level routes:

```js
const RESERVED_SLUGS = new Set([
  'api', 'admin', 'auth', 'login', 'logout', 'signup',
  'health', 'static', 'assets', 'docs', 'support',
  'www', 'mail', 'ftp', 'blog', 'help', 'status',
]);
```

### 3.4 Pros
- Works on day one with zero infrastructure changes
- Preview/staging environments work identically to production
- Standard Express routing — no middleware magic
- SEO: all content on one domain, full link equity
- CORS: single `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` value
- Cookies: no cross-domain complexity

### 3.5 Cons
- URLs are longer and less "premium" feeling
- Club admins can't point a custom domain easily (possible but adds complexity)
- Multi-tenant isolation is purely logical, not at the network layer

---

## 4. Subdomain-Based: Deep Dive

### 4.1 DNS Setup

You need a wildcard DNS record:
```
Type: A
Name: *.app.com
Value: <your server IP>
TTL: 300
```

This is a single record but it must be set at your DNS provider (Cloudflare, Route 53, etc.) and it takes time to propagate. Every new club slug automatically resolves — no per-club DNS record needed.

### 4.2 TLS Certificate Options

**Option A: Wildcard cert via Let's Encrypt + Certbot**
```bash
certbot certonly \
  --dns-cloudflare \
  --dns-cloudflare-credentials /etc/cloudflare.ini \
  -d "*.app.com" \
  -d "app.com"
```
- Requires DNS challenge (not HTTP challenge) — needs API access to your DNS provider
- Wildcard cert covers all subdomains but must be renewed every 90 days
- Does NOT auto-provision per-new-club — the wildcard already covers them

**Option B: Per-club ACME via `node-acme-client`**

Only needed if clubs bring their own custom domains (`club.their-own-domain.com`). Massive added complexity — skip for v1.

**Option C: Cloudflare Proxy**

If you're behind Cloudflare, Cloudflare terminates TLS and issues the wildcard cert for you automatically. Your origin only needs a self-signed cert (or Cloudflare Origin cert). Zero cert management.

### 4.3 Nginx Config for Wildcard Subdomain Routing

```nginx
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name ~^(?<slug>[a-z0-9-]+)\.app\.com$;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/app.com/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/app.com/privkey.pem;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:3000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Club-Slug $slug;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    }
}
```

### 4.4 Express Middleware for Subdomain Extraction

```js
function extractClubFromSubdomain(req, res, next) {
  const host = req.hostname; // e.g., "the-pines-gc.app.com"
  const baseDomain = process.env.BASE_DOMAIN; // "app.com"

  if (!host.endsWith(`.${baseDomain}`)) {
    return next(); // main app, no club context
  }

  const slug = host.slice(0, -(baseDomain.length + 1));
  if (!slug || slug === 'www') return next();

  req.clubSlug = slug;
  next();
}

app.use(extractClubFromSubdomain);
```

Or read from the header Nginx injected:
```js
req.clubSlug = req.headers['x-club-slug'];
```

### 4.5 CORS Complexity

With subdomains, `club-a.app.com` and `club-b.app.com` are different origins. If your SPA on the main domain calls a club subdomain API, you need CORS.

```js
app.use(cors({
  origin: (origin, callback) => {
    if (!origin) return callback(null, true); // curl/server-side
    const url = new URL(origin);
    if (url.hostname === 'app.com' || url.hostname.endsWith('.app.com')) {
      return callback(null, true);
    }
    callback(new Error('Not allowed by CORS'));
  },
  credentials: true,
}));
```

### 4.6 Cookie/Session Sharing

If a user logs in at `app.com` and then visits `the-pines-gc.app.com`, their session cookie won't be sent unless it was set with `Domain=.app.com`.

```js
req.session.cookie.domain = '.app.com'; // note leading dot
```

This works for subdomains of your own domain but has security implications: any subdomain can read the cookie. If a club subdomain were ever compromised or XSS'd, it could access the session. Mitigate by:
- Using `__Host-` or `__Secure-` prefixed cookies (strips domain scope)
- Issuing per-club JWT tokens scoped to that subdomain
- Validating club membership server-side on every request regardless

### 4.7 Local Development Workarounds

`localhost` doesn't support subdomains natively. Options:

**Option 1: `/etc/hosts` (manual, per-club)**
```
127.0.0.1  the-pines-gc.localhost
127.0.0.1  riverside-cc.localhost
```
Tedious for development; doesn't scale.

**Option 2: `dnsmasq` (wildcard local DNS)**
```bash
brew install dnsmasq
echo 'address=/.localhost/127.0.0.1' >> /usr/local/etc/dnsmasq.conf
sudo brew services start dnsmasq
```
Then add `127.0.0.1` as a DNS resolver for `.localhost` in macOS Network settings. Now `anything.localhost` resolves locally.

**Option 3: Use a different local strategy**
Fall back to path-based routing in development and subdomain in production. Use an env flag:

```js
function getClubSlug(req) {
  if (process.env.ROUTING_STRATEGY === 'subdomain') {
    return extractFromSubdomain(req);
  }
  return req.params.slug; // path-based fallback
}
```

### 4.8 Pros
- Premium UX: `the-pines-gc.app.com` looks polished
- Clear multi-tenant isolation visible at the network layer
- Natural foundation for future custom domain mapping
- Per-club security boundaries (separate cookies possible)

### 4.9 Cons
- Requires wildcard DNS + wildcard TLS cert before you can run in production
- Cookie and CORS complexity is real and easy to get wrong
- Local dev requires workarounds
- Preview/staging environments on Railway, Render, Fly.io etc. don't support wildcard subdomains on their auto-generated hostnames
- SEO: each subdomain is a separate site to search engines — distributed link equity, harder to build domain authority

---

## 5. Migration Path: Path → Subdomain

This is the recommended approach. Ship path-based, migrate later.

### 5.1 Step 1: Design routes with migration in mind

Use a middleware abstraction from day one:

```js
// lib/club-context.js
async function resolveClubContext(req, res, next) {
  let slug;

  // Future: subdomain support
  const host = req.hostname;
  if (process.env.SUBDOMAIN_ROUTING === 'true') {
    const base = process.env.BASE_DOMAIN;
    if (host.endsWith(`.${base}`)) {
      slug = host.slice(0, -(base.length + 1));
    }
  }

  // Current: path-based
  if (!slug) {
    slug = req.params.slug;
  }

  if (!slug) return next();

  const club = await Club.findBySlug(slug);
  if (!club) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'Club not found' });

  req.club = club;
  next();
}

module.exports = { resolveClubContext };
```

### 5.2 Step 2: Add redirects when subdomains go live

```js
// Redirect old path-based URLs to subdomain URLs
router.get('/clubs/:slug*', (req, res) => {
  if (process.env.SUBDOMAIN_ROUTING === 'true') {
    const rest = req.params[0] || '';
    return res.redirect(301,
      `https://${req.params.slug}.${process.env.BASE_DOMAIN}${rest}`
    );
  }
  next();
});
```

### 5.3 Step 3: Enable via env flag

```bash
SUBDOMAIN_ROUTING=true
BASE_DOMAIN=app.com
```

Flip the flag after DNS + cert infrastructure is in place. No code deploy required for the switch.

---

## 6. Custom Domain Support (Future)

If clubs eventually want `scores.their-club.com` pointing to your app, that's a separate problem:

1. Club admin sets a CNAME: `scores.their-club.com → app.com`
2. Your server receives the request with `Host: scores.their-club.com`
3. You look up which club owns that custom domain in your DB
4. You provision a TLS cert for `scores.their-club.com` via ACME HTTP challenge

This is a non-trivial feature. Tools that help:
- `@acmejs/client` or `node-acme-client` npm packages for ACME
- Caddy as a reverse proxy (auto-HTTPS including for custom domains via its `on_demand_tls` feature)
- Traefik with Let's Encrypt integration

Don't design for this in v1. The path-based approach doesn't block it — custom domain support is additive regardless of your internal routing strategy.

---

## 7. Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Path-Based | Subdomain |
|---|---|---|
| Time to ship (no DNS infra) | Hours | Days–weeks |
| Works on preview/staging URLs | Yes | No |
| Local dev complexity | None | Medium |
| Cookie complexity | None | Medium |
| CORS complexity | None | Low–medium |
| "Premium" URL look | No | Yes |
| Future custom domain support | Additive | Natural fit |
| SEO (single domain) | Better | Worse |
| Operational overhead | Minimal | Cert renewal + wildcard DNS |

---

## 8. Recommendation

**Phase 1 (ship now):** Path-based routing at `/clubs/:slug`. Takes hours, not days. Wrap the slug resolution in a `resolveClubContext` middleware abstraction so the routing strategy is an implementation detail your handlers never see.

**Phase 2 (after first clubs are live):** Add wildcard DNS and a wildcard Let's Encrypt cert. Flip `SUBDOMAIN_ROUTING=true`. Add 301 redirects from old paths.

**Phase 3 (later, if demand):** Custom domain support via ACME on-demand cert provisioning. Caddy makes this nearly turnkey if you're willing to use it as your reverse proxy.

The abstraction cost of doing path-based first is essentially zero — one middleware function. The infrastructure cost of doing subdomain-based first is real and blocks you from using preview environments and complicates every dev environment setup on your team.
